one_line_takeaway: MIAs largely fail against tabular ML models under realistic conditions, but uniquely identifiable ('single-out') records remain highly exposed even to otherwise weak attacks, and attackers using mismatched surrogate model architectures can still transfer effective attacks without needing knowledge of the true target architecture.
key_finding: Contrary to prior literature (largely established on image data or complex tabular tasks), MIAs perform poorly on simple tabular data: even overfitted target models are only marginally more vulnerable than a random-guess baseline (50% accuracy/AUC), while well-generalized models show little to no leakage under model-based attacks. Reference-model-based attacks (LiRA, RMIA) consistently outperform shadow-model-based attacks, with Online LiRA dominant in AUC across datasets, though other reference-based attacks are more effective at fixed low FPR (TPR@1%FPR). Random Forest, XGBoost, and Decision Trees are more vulnerable than Naive Bayes, Logistic Regression, and Neural Networks. Applying hyper-parameter tuning/regularization as a defense generally reduces attack efficacy without materially harming target accuracy, and FL can act as an incidental defense for some models (e.g., XGBoost) but not uniformly across all. The relationship between overfitting (generalization gap) and MIA vulnerability is not strictly linear -- e.g., Random Forest on the Locations dataset has a 41% generalization gap but only ~50.69% AUC under Online LiRA, while Dropout Success has a smaller 12.6% gap but ~60% AUC. Despite this weak overall attack performance, single-out records are disproportionately exposed: on XGBoost without defenses, detection rates range from 43.33% (Online RMIA, Covid) up to 100.0% (Online and Offline RMIA on both Dropout Success and Half Million), with Online RMIA reaching exactly 100.0% on both Half Million and Dropout Success, showing that even globally weak attacks can still expose a large share of uniquely identifiable individuals. Finally, transferability experiments on Cirrhosis show that many attack-model/target-model combinations using mismatched (different) surrogate architectures outperform the matched-architecture baseline, indicating that adversaries can mount effective MIAs without knowledge of the exact target architecture -- for example, Offline RMIA with an XGBoost surrogate attacking a Random Forest target reaches 65.88% AUC, exceeding the corresponding baseline diagonal values.
core_contribution: This is a Systematization of Knowledge (SoK) on Membership Inference Attacks (MIAs) against models trained on tabular data, covering both centralized and federated learning (FL). The paper's own contributions (as distinct from the surveyed related work) are: (1) a new taxonomy for MIAs in both centralized and FL settings, organized by the type of information the adversary can access (target output vector vs. target model internals) rather than the conventional black-box/white-box split; (2) an empirical evaluation of multiple MIA families (shadow-model-based, reference-model-based [LiRA online/offline, RMIA online/offline], and metric-based/model-free attacks such as prediction correctness, confidence, loss, and modified entropy) across nine tabular datasets (Adult, Cirrhosis, Covid, Dropout Success, Gallstone, Half Million, Locations, Purchases-20, Student Performance) and six ML model types (Naive Bayes, Logistic Regression, Decision Trees, Random Forest, XGBoost, Neural Network), with and without defenses (hyper-parameter tuning/regularization) and under FL as an implicit defense; (3) the first evaluation (per the authors) of MIA vulnerability specifically of 'single-out' records (individuals uniquely identifiable via quasi-identifiers, identified via a k-anonymity-style frequency count fk=1) as opposed to generic outliers; and (4) an empirical study of MIA transferability, i.e. attack model performance when the attacker's surrogate/shadow model architecture differs from the target model's architecture (Adversary 2, no direct target access), tested pairwise across the six model types on the Cirrhosis dataset.
threat_model: The paper formalizes MIA as the standard membership-inference security game (challenger samples a training set S, trains f_theta, flips a bit to select a member or non-member point, and sends the target model and point to the adversary, who outputs a membership guess). Two knowledge levels are used empirically: Adversary 1, who has additional knowledge of the target model's architecture and the distribution of its training data (used for the main efficacy and single-out experiments, i.e., a white-box/architecture-aware setting); and Adversary 2, who knows the data distribution but has no access to the target model itself and must rely on a surrogate/shadow model of possibly different architecture (used for the transferability experiments). In the FL setting the paper explicitly adopts an 'outsider' adversary -- one with access only to the final global model rather than to participants' gradients/updates -- which the authors argue is understudied relative to the more commonly modeled 'insider' (participant or aggregator) adversary. All attacks target black-box access to the output vector (predicted label or class probabilities); attacks requiring internal model access (gradients, hidden-layer activations) are surveyed in the literature review but not part of the paper's own experiments.
limitations: The authors' own single-out results are presented for only one model (XGBoost) and three of the nine datasets 'for the sake of conciseness,' so generality across all model/dataset combinations is not directly demonstrated. Reference-model-based attacks (LiRA, RMIA) are described as costly since they typically require training multiple reference models, unlike shadow attacks which the authors show can work with a single shadow model. Metric-based (model-free) attacks are noted to be inapplicable to true MLaaS black-box settings because they require access to ground-truth labels, restricting their practical use to pre-deployment auditing rather than live API attacks. The paper explicitly scopes out active attacks such as poisoning or adversarial-example-based MIA enhancement, and restricts the FL analysis to horizontal FL (HFL), leaving vertical FL (VFL) MIA evaluation for future/open work, citing VFL's relative immaturity. The transferability analysis is conducted on a single dataset (Cirrhosis) rather than across all nine datasets. The paper also flags that TPR/FPR-based conclusions (e.g., LiRA dominance) can differ from AUC-based conclusions, complicating claims of a single 'best' attack.

quantitative_results: Random Forest and XGBoost show median attack AUC above 55% under LiRA/RMIA in centralized learning without defenses, higher than other model types
Single-out detection on XGBoost (no defense): Shadow via Predictions = 99.7% (Covid), 62.5% (Dropout Success), 74.25% (Half Million)
Single-out detection on XGBoost (no defense): Online RMIA = 43.33% (Covid), 100.0% (Dropout Success), 100.0% (Half Million)
Single-out detection on XGBoost (no defense): Offline RMIA = 60.91% (Covid), 100.0% (Dropout Success), 99.69% (Half Million)
Random Forest on Locations dataset: 41% generalization gap but only ~50.69% AUC under Online LiRA
Dropout Success dataset: ~12.6% generalization gap but ~60% AUC
Metric-based attacks on XGBoost/Naive Bayes show MIA Advantage (TPR-FPR) median between roughly 65% and 70% for some attacks
Cirrhosis transferability matrix (Table 3): Offline RMIA with XGBoost-surrogate attacking Random Forest target reaches 65.88% AUC, exceeding several matched-architecture baseline values
baselines_compared: Shadow via Predictions (Shokri et al. shadow model attack)
Metric-based model-free attacks: prediction correctness, confidence threshold, loss threshold, modified entropy
